 title: "Ebony Orchid Problem v11.37"

  description: "At 22:30, the Ebony Orchid disappeared from its sealed container in the Arboretum Annex. Four individuals (1, 2, 3, 4) had valid clearance. Exactly one suspect lies in both statements; the remaining three are fully truthful. Only one suspect is the real culprit. Each suspect gives two statements:

    Suspect 1:
      (1) 'I left at 22:20, so I could not have stolen the Orchid.'
      (2) 'Suspect 4 was still near the container at 22:25.'

    Suspect 2:
      (1) 'Suspect 1 actually stayed until 22:35.'
      (2) 'If 3 is lying, then 4 is also lying.'

    Suspect 3:
      (1) 'I never went near the Orchid after 22:15.'
      (2) 'Either 2 is guilty, or 1 lied about departure — not both.'

    Suspect 4:
      (1) 'Suspect 2 was exactly outside the container at 22:25.'
      (2) 'I am innocent, and 1’s early exit is correct.'

  question: "Which suspect is guilty? Provide one digit (1–4)."

answer: >
  explanation: "Formal deduction:
  Let L(x) mean 'x lies in both statements', T(x) mean 'x is fully truthful', and G(x) mean 'x is guilty'.
  Only one x satisfies L(x), only one x is G(x)=true, others are T(x).
  
  Case L(1):
    Then 1(1),(2) false => 1 did NOT leave 22:20, 4 not near container 22:25. 2,3,4 are truthful. 2(1) '1 stayed 22:35' must be correct. Checking times leads to contradictions with 4(1). Fails.
  
  Case L(2):
    Then 2(1),(2) false => 1 indeed left 22:20, 2's 'If 3 is lying => 4 is lying' is false. Then 1,3,4 are truthful. 3(2) 'Either 2 is guilty or 1 lied, not both' => with 1 truthful => 2 is guilty. 4(2) 'I am innocent, 1’s exit correct' => consistent. So L(2) => G(2).
  
  Case L(3):
    Then 3(1),(2) false => 3 did approach after 22:15, etc. 1,2,4 truthful triggers timing mismatches. Contradiction.
  
  Case L(4):
    Then 4(1),(2) false => 1,2,3 truthful. More timeline conflicts about who saw who. Contradiction.

  Only L(2) scenario is coherent => suspect 2 is also guilty.
  final: '2'"
